Work

Projects at the intersection of technology, community, and language — from nonprofit engineering to neuroscience research to civic poetry.

NONPROFIT

Play-Based Therapy Kits for Children Who Have Experienced Trauma

By Karan Gupta ·2023-11 ·San Francisco, CA

PlayKits began from a simple observation: children who have experienced trauma need more than academic support — they need safe, structured opportunities to play, create, and reconnect with their own sense of agency. Founded in 2022, PlayKits designs and distributes curated therapy kits that support children's emotional recovery through guided play-based activities.

Each kit is developed in consultation with child trauma specialists and play therapists, containing materials and facilitation guides for activities proven to reduce stress responses, build emotional regulation, and restore a sense of safety and control. Kits are distributed through partner schools, pediatric clinics, foster care networks, and community mental health organizations.

To date, PlayKits has served over 500 children across the Bay Area. The organization partners with social workers, school counselors, and pediatric care providers who identify children in need and ensure kits reach them with appropriate support. In 2024, PlayKits expanded its model to include caregiver guides, recognizing that durable healing happens when the adults in a child's life are equipped to support ongoing play-based recovery at home.

[BODY PARAGRAPH PLACEHOLDER — expand with specific impact metrics, partner quotes, and program logistics]

"Play is not a break from healing. Play is how children heal."

— PlayKits.org

Update Log

2024-09

Expanded distribution through three new pediatric partner clinics.

2024-06

Reached 500 children served milestone.

2024-03

Launched caregiver guide series in partnership with trauma therapists.

PlayKits — Play-based therapy kits in action

NONPROFIT

PlayKits.org

PlayKits is a nonprofit providing play-based therapy kits to children who have undergone trauma — because healing and play are not separate things.
RESEARCH

Adaptive Learning Interfaces at the Intersection of Neuroscience and Technology

By Karan Gupta ·2023-12 ·San Francisco, CA

At Neuroscape, a translational neuroscience center at UCSF, I work on the intersection of cognitive neuroscience and human-computer interaction. The lab's core mission is to develop technologies that can detect, track, and modify brain states — with applications ranging from ADHD treatment to accelerated skill acquisition.

My work focuses specifically on adaptive learning interfaces: systems that change their presentation, pacing, and complexity in response to real-time biometric signals. Using EEG-derived attention metrics and pupillometry, we prototype educational software that "reads" a learner's cognitive load and adjusts accordingly.

The research draws on principles from closed-loop neurostimulation, applied machine learning, and pedagogical design theory. A key challenge is latency: cognitive adaptation must happen faster than conscious awareness to avoid disrupting flow states.

[BODY PARAGRAPH PLACEHOLDER — expand with specific experiments, results, and publication status]

"The most powerful educational technology is one the student never notices — it just works."

— UCSF Neuroscape

Update Log

2024-10

Pilot study manuscript submitted for peer review.

2024-07

Presented findings at Cognitive Neuroscience Society Annual Meeting.

2024-04

IRB approval received for expanded participant cohort.

Neuroscape — Adaptive interface prototype demo

RESEARCH

UCSF Neuroscape

Research at UCSF's Neuroscape lab focuses on building neurofeedback tools that adapt in real-time to a learner's cognitive state.
ENGINEERING

Teaching Facilities Workers to Use Robots — Deployed at Apple, Sutter Health, Cadence, and Whole Foods

By Karan Gupta ·2024-03 ·San Francisco, CA

Cardinal Robotics deploys autonomous service robots to enterprise facilities. As Tech Lead, my role sits at the intersection of robotics engineering and human-centered training: I design and run the onboarding programs that teach janitorial and facilities staff how to work alongside the robots we deploy.

The core insight driving this work is that a robot is only as useful as the humans who can direct, correct, and collaborate with it. Rather than positioning automation as a replacement for workers, our model treats frontline staff as expert operators — people who understand the specific terrain of their building better than any pre-programmed map.

To date, the program has deployed at Apple, Sutter Health, Cadence Design Systems, and Whole Foods. Each deployment involves custom training protocols tailored to the specific environment and workflow, co-developed with the workers themselves. Early feedback consistently shows that staff who receive proper training report higher job satisfaction and feel more, not less, in control of their work.

[BODY PARAGRAPH PLACEHOLDER — add specific deployment details, training curriculum, and measurable efficiency outcomes]

"The robot is a tool. The janitor is the expert. My job is to make sure they know it."

— Cardinal Robotics

Update Log

2024-11

Deployment completed at Whole Foods regional distribution center.

2024-09

Cadence Design Systems pilot program expanded to two additional campuses.

2024-06

Sutter Health deployment — 40 facilities staff trained across three sites.

Cardinal Robotics — Facilities staff training program

ENGINEERING

Cardinal Robotics

As Tech Lead at Cardinal Robotics, I train janitorial and facilities staff to work alongside autonomous cleaning robots — making their work more efficient without replacing their expertise.
COMMUNITY

Appointed San Francisco's Youth Poet Laureate: A Year of Verse in Service of the City

By Karan Gupta ·2024-01 ·San Francisco, CA

The San Francisco Youth Poet Laureate program, administered by Youth Speaks, appoints one young poet annually to serve as a literary ambassador for the city. I was appointed by Mayor Daniel Lurie, and the role has taken me into rooms and stages I could not have imagined: from the Super Bowl press conference to KQED studios to late-night community workshops with teenagers who have never thought of themselves as writers.

My tenure centered on three themes: the geography of displacement as San Francisco's neighborhoods transform, the experience of growing up between cultures, and the quiet heroisms of everyday life in a city that too often forgets its working-class residents.

I work in close partnership with Youth Speaks — the Bay Area's leading spoken word education organization — and 826 Valencia, the nonprofit writing and tutoring center on Valencia Street. Together, these partnerships have allowed me to reach young writers across the city, from public school classrooms to community centers, delivering workshops that treat poetry not as a school subject but as a survival skill.

[BODY PARAGRAPH PLACEHOLDER — add specific poems written during tenure, event highlights, and lasting impact]

"A city is only as alive as the words its people use to name it."

— SF Youth Poet Laureate

Update Log

2024-12

Featured on KQED and FOX News California as SF Youth Poet Laureate.

2024-10

Performed at Super Bowl press conference.

2024-09

Led workshop series with 826 Valencia at three community sites.

SF Youth Poet Laureate — Inaugural reading at City Hall

COMMUNITY

SF Youth Poet Laureate

Appointed SF Youth Poet Laureate by Mayor Daniel Lurie — performing at the Super Bowl press conference, KQED, FOX News California, and leading workshops with Youth Speaks and 826 Valencia.
WRITING

Essays, Dispatches, and Criticism from the Intersection of Technology and Humanity

By Karan Gupta ·2024-04 ·San Francisco, CA

My nonfiction writing sits at the intersection of technology criticism, personal essay, and urban reportage. I'm interested in the stories that fall through the cracks of mainstream coverage: the tutoring center displaced by a tech office, the grandmother who learned English from Siri, the teenager for whom TikTok is the primary source of political education.

Recent work has appeared in The Bold Italic, Youth Voices magazine, and the Stanford Daily. I maintain a Substack newsletter, "Signal/Noise," exploring the attention economy, digital literacy, and the ethics of design.

[BODY PARAGRAPH PLACEHOLDER — add specific essay titles, publications, and key arguments]

"The best technology criticism is indistinguishable from the best human writing."

— Writing & Publications

Update Log

2024-10

"The Loneliness Algorithm" published in The Bold Italic.

2024-07

Signal/Noise newsletter reaches 800 subscribers.

2024-04

Essay on AI tutoring tools cited in EdWeek article.

Writing — Signal/Noise Newsletter

WRITING

Writing & Publications

Published essays exploring how technology reshapes community, identity, and what it means to be young in a city that's changing faster than anyone can document.
